text: Brampton of land—Distribntion of compensation money—Tenancy m common—Improvements effected by predecessor in title of one of tenants in common— Equitable rigitof euch tenant to compensation for improvements on distribution—Defensive 'equity Effect when Court not administering whole fund—-Public Works Act (N.S. (20. 26 of 1900), secs. 39, 47, 48 The equitable right of a tenant in common of real estate, who has made permanent improvements upon it while in sole occupation, to be compensated for his expenditure to the extent to which the value of the land has been inteased thereby, is one which attaches to the land and passes with it to a Purchaser, but is enforceable only as a defensive equity, in the event of a Patitio, or a distribution, amongst the tenants in common, by the Court of Euity, of the proceeds of sale of the land. 'The fact that the tenant in common has already received without objection "portion of the fund does not necessarily prevent the application of this Principle, 'The appellant was for several years in exclusive possession and enjoyment af the rents and profits of certain land, of which he believed himself to have Yarehased the fee simple, whereas in fact he had acquired only an estate pur ure vie of the entirety, with an equitable tenancy in common with certain of therespondents in remainder, his share being one fourth. Permanent imMovements had been erected upon the land by one of the appellant's predeces'orsin title, After the death of the cestui qui vie the land was resumed by the Minister for Public Works, under the Public Works Act (N.8.W.) 1900. 'The Mibister paid the appellant one fourth of the compensation moneys, and, at erequest of the appellant, paid the balance into Court, 'The appellant H.C. or A. 1905. — Sypyey, March 20, 21. April 14, Gritith O., Barton and O'Connor JS.

text: The following statement of the facts is taken from the judgma of Grifith CJ.:— This appeal relates to the distribution of a sum of money into Court by the Minister for Publie Works, representing value of land resumed by the Crown for public purposes 18th May, 1869, the appellant's predecessor in title, one Gannon six persons, Elizabeth Young and her five children, who were of full age. Elizabeth Young was tenant for life of the under her deceased husband's will, and it appears to have b assumed that the other five vendors were tenants in common it remainder. In fact, however, they had only executory int the actual trusts of the will having been for the use of the for life, and after her death for such of the testator's children as should be living at her decease, but the testator directed that, in the children died unmarried.

text: Three of the others died in lifetime of the tenant for life, leaving issue, who consequen took the shares of their deceased parents. One of his predecessors in title, one Porter, had in 1872 erected houses upon the land, by which it was alleged that its value was considerably increased.

text: The equity is a lien on the estate itself: Story's Equi Jurisprudence, see. 1234, et seq. It is not to be confined suits for partition, it is to be applied to any ease in which property has been sold by order of the Court, or the proceeds its sale are being distributed by the Court: Leigh v. Dickesm(l) 'The person who claims the equity need not be the person who actually expended the money.

text: QCoxxor J. [Having shortly stated the facts His Honor _preeeded,] It was admitted by the respondents' counsel that if the appellant had during his possession of the land made the improvements, and if the one fourth share of the compensation money had not been paid over to him, he would have been entitled tobe recouped to the extent claimed, less certain deductions which shall mention later on; but they contended that under the cireumstances existing there were two fatal obstacles in his way—the fist, that the moneys disbursed for improvements had been expended not by him but by his predecessor in title, who had been rvimbursed for that expenditure in the purchase money he had neeived. 'The second, that the appellant's receipt of the one fourth af the compensation money was a severing of the tenancy in cmmon which disentitled him from making this claim in the distribution of the fund. As to the first objection, I entirely agree with my learned nother the Chief Justice in his statement of the principles to be applied in such circumstances as this, namely, that the making of Jetmanent improvements by one tenant in common in sole oceupation gives rise to an equity attaching to the land analogous to an tquitable charge created by the owners for the time being, awaitlng enforcement pending partition or distribution of the value of theland as against the tenants in common. If the resumption had taken place during the possession of the person, who actually 'pended money on the improvements, the respondents would fave been bound to recoup him.
citation: Brickwood v Young and Minister for Public Works (NSW) [1905] HCA 12

text: At the date of the resumption the appellant was tenant in common with the respondents in the land resumed, and his inte have been bound, apart from other objections, to give effect both of those rights. I do not see how the appellant ean lose of them—the right to reimbursement out of that portion of compensation money—which belongs to the respondents, beean before the money had been paid into Court he accepted, wit prejudice to his rights, that portion of the compensation which admittedly belonged to him. In other words the rights d all parties interested are fixed and stereotyped as at the date o resumption, and the compensation money must be distributed in accordance with those tights subject to proper allowances for part payments if any made before final adjustment.
citation: Brickwood v Young and Minister for Public Works (NSW) [1905] HCA 12

text: In my view, therefore, the appellant was entitled to make his claim for te imbursement, notwithstanding the fact that the improvements were made by his predecessor in title, and that he was paid his fourth share of the compensation before the fund was paid Court. I agree with the Chief Justice that the principle down by Lord Justice Cotton in Leigh v. Dickeson (1), and which has been applied in the distribution of the fund in partitions aat administration, ought to be applied to the case of a distributional compensation for land resumed under the Public Works Act, Iti true that the principle applied as what is called a defensive equity, but it is properly raised here. 'The respondents in this appeal the moving parties in this proceeding for distributing thefund.

In order to effectually exercise this 'git the appellant is entitled to the inquiry he asks, and I with the Chief Justice that it should be on the lines ition precedent to his right to the inquiry claimed by him. as it might tum out on the inquiry that the amount of sand profits for which the appellant is accountable to the sspondents exceeds the amount of reimbursement for improveAppeal allowed.
